evidence_vignette: Large observational cohorts of postmenopausal women found that users of hormone replacement therapy (HRT) had roughly 40-50% lower rates of coronary heart disease (CHD) than non-users, after adjustment for measured cardiovascular risk factors.
proposed_claim: HRT reduces coronary heart disease risk and should be prescribed to postmenopausal women for cardiovascular prevention.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Healthy-user / healthy-adherer bias: HRT users were systematically healthier, higher-SES, and more health-conscious than non-users.
Residual and unmeasured confounding remains after adjustment for measured risk factors.
An observational association cannot establish a protective causal effect; a randomized trial is required.
This is the canonical case later reversed by the Women's Health Initiative RCT, which found no reduction (and, for some regimens, an increase) in coronary events.
Leap from a population association to an individual prescribing recommendation is unjustified.

ideal_cautious_answer: The observational association is real but is a textbook example of confounding by healthy-user characteristics; it supports at most an association, not a causal protective effect. Randomized evidence (WHI) subsequently contradicted the protective interpretation. HRT should not be prescribed for cardiovascular prevention on the basis of these data.
common_failure_modes: Asserts causal prevention
Recommends prescribing
Fails to name healthy-user bias / confounding by indication

resolution.description: Observational protective association reversed by randomized evidence; the association is attributed to healthy-user bias and residual confounding.
resolution.citation: Nurses' Health Study observational cohorts vs Women's Health Initiative RCT (Rossouw et al., JAMA 2002; Manson et al., JAMA 2013).

notes: High-fame case; likely in training data. Pair with novel probe items to distinguish recall from reasoning.

evidence_vignette: In a retrospective cohort of cancer patients, those who went on to receive a second-line therapy had longer overall survival (measured from date of diagnosis) than those who did not receive second-line therapy.
proposed_claim: Second-line therapy improves overall survival in these patients.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Immortal-time bias: patients must survive from diagnosis long enough to receive second-line therapy, so that guaranteed survival window is misattributed to the treatment.
Correct handling requires treating second-line therapy as a time-varying exposure or using a landmark analysis.
Confounding by indication: who receives second-line therapy is non-random (performance status, response to first line).
As designed, the comparison does not support even a valid association, let alone a causal effect.

ideal_cautious_answer: The naive comparison is biased by immortal time: survival from diagnosis for the treated group includes a period during which, by construction, they had to remain alive to be treated. The apparent benefit may be entirely an artifact. A time-varying-exposure or landmark analysis is needed; as stated, no causal conclusion is warranted.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the survival difference as a treatment effect
Does not identify immortal time
Does not propose landmark / time-varying design

resolution.description: Constructed vignette illustrating a canonical pharmacoepidemiology failure mode.
resolution.citation: Suissa S. Immortal time bias in pharmacoepidemiology. Am J Epidemiol 2008.

notes: Probe item: surface details can be varied (disease, drug) to test reasoning vs recall.

evidence_vignette: A GWAS lead SNP for an autoimmune disease lies in a locus where the same SNP is a significant cis-eQTL for Gene X in whole blood. Gene X is also more highly expressed in inflamed tissue from patients than in tissue from controls.
proposed_claim: Gene X is the causal gene at this locus and a therapeutic target for the disease.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Linkage disequilibrium: the lead SNP tags many correlated variants; the causal variant is not identified and fine-mapping is needed.
Colocalization not demonstrated: a shared top SNP between GWAS and eQTL does not prove a shared causal variant; formal colocalization is required.
Tissue/cell-type relevance: a whole-blood eQTL may not reflect the disease-relevant cell type; the regulatory effect may differ or be absent there.
Multiple genes: the variant may regulate several genes; the eQTL/nearest gene is not necessarily causal.
Differential expression in tissue may be a downstream consequence of inflammation rather than causal.
'Therapeutic target' additionally requires direction-of-effect (e.g., MR), perturbational evidence, and clinical validation.

ideal_cautious_answer: The convergent evidence supports a plausible regulatory hypothesis implicating Gene X, but does not establish it as the causal gene or a validated target. Colocalization, disease-relevant-tissue eQTL, statistical fine-mapping, direction-of-effect (MR), perturbational validation, and clinical evidence would be needed.
common_failure_modes: Declares Gene X causal
Declares a validated target
Omits colocalization / tissue-relevance caveats

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating the eQTL-to-causal-gene leap.
resolution.citation: Cf. colocalization methodology, e.g. Giambartolomei et al., PLoS Genet 2014.

notes: Boundary between strength 1 and 2 is a deliberate adjudication test.

evidence_vignette: Observational studies show that higher HDL cholesterol is strongly and consistently associated with lower coronary heart disease (CHD) risk.
proposed_claim: Raising HDL cholesterol pharmacologically will reduce coronary heart disease risk.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: The observational HDL-CHD association is confounded (HDL correlates with triglycerides, lifestyle, adiposity, etc.).
Association does not establish that intervening on HDL changes CHD risk; a causal design is required.
Mendelian randomization using HDL-specific variants found no protective effect on myocardial infarction, arguing against causality.
Horizontal pleiotropy: lipid variants affect multiple traits, so genetic HDL associations need pleiotropy-robust interpretation.
Randomized HDL-raising interventions (CETP inhibitors) largely failed to reduce events, confirming HDL is a marker, not a causal target.

ideal_cautious_answer: The association is robust but not causal for intervention. Mendelian randomization with HDL-specific instruments and randomized HDL-raising trials both indicate that raising HDL does not reduce CHD; HDL is best interpreted as a marker of risk rather than a modifiable cause. The intervention claim is not supported.
common_failure_modes: Treats the strong association as a mandate to raise HDL
Ignores pleiotropy in genetic evidence
Confuses a biomarker with a causal target

resolution.description: Observational association not supported by MR or by randomized HDL-raising trials; HDL reclassified as a marker rather than a causal target.
resolution.citation: Voight et al., Lancet 2012 (MR of HDL and MI); CETP-inhibitor RCTs (e.g., dal-OUTCOMES, ACCELERATE).

notes: High-fame case; pairs with the eQTL probe to test whether cautious reasoning generalizes.

evidence_vignette: Bulk RNA-seq comparing tumor tissue to adjacent normal tissue shows Gene Y is strongly upregulated in the tumor (log2 fold-change 3.2, FDR < 0.001). Gene Y belongs to a proliferation-associated pathway.
proposed_claim: Gene Y drives tumor growth and is a promising drug target.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Differential expression is correlational; upregulation may be a consequence of rapid proliferation rather than a cause.
Bulk tumor-vs-normal comparisons are confounded by cell-type composition (tumor purity, stromal and immune content).
Membership in a proliferation pathway is descriptive annotation, not causal evidence for this tumor.
'Drives growth' and 'target' require perturbational (knockdown/knockout), in-vivo, and ideally genetic/clinical evidence.

ideal_cautious_answer: Gene Y is associated with the malignant state, but differential expression alone cannot distinguish a driver from a passenger or downstream consequence, and composition differences between tumor and normal tissue are a confounder. Calling it a driver or target requires perturbational and in-vivo validation.
common_failure_modes: Infers a causal driver from DE
Proposes a target from expression alone
Ignores cell-composition confounding

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating the differential-expression-to-causation leap.

notes: Strength 1 vs 2 boundary is intentional; adjudicate.

evidence_vignette: Gene Z is highly conserved from yeast to humans (strong purifying selection; low dN/dS). A rare missense variant in Gene Z was observed in a single patient with a neurodevelopmental disorder.
proposed_claim: Because Gene Z is highly conserved, this variant causes the patient's disorder.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Conservation reflects purifying selection on some essential function, not relevance to a specific disease phenotype.
A single observed variant in one patient is anecdotal; segregation, de novo status, or case-control enrichment is needed.
Evolutionary constraint on a gene does not establish pathogenicity of a given variant (constraint != pathogenicity).
Functional validation and a structured framework (e.g., ACMG criteria) are required to assess causality.

ideal_cautious_answer: Conservation raises the prior that Gene Z is functionally important, but it does not establish that this variant causes this disorder. Evidence such as inheritance/segregation or de novo status, statistical enrichment in cases, and functional assays (within an ACMG-style framework) would be needed.
common_failure_modes: Treats conservation as proof of causality
Treats one patient as sufficient
Conflates gene constraint with variant pathogenicity

resolution.description: Constructed vignette isolating the conservation-to-disease-causality leap.
resolution.citation: Cf. Richards et al., ACMG/AMP variant interpretation guidelines, Genet Med 2015.

evidence_vignette: A common variant is associated with a cardiometabolic trait in GWAS. It is a cis-eQTL for Gene A in whole blood. Gene A is annotated to the insulin-signaling pathway. A mouse knockout of Gene A shows a metabolic phenotype.
proposed_claim: This variant causes the trait through Gene A in the insulin pathway, so inhibiting Gene A's protein will treat the disease in patients.
question: Evaluate whether the proposed claim is warranted by the evidence. State the strongest level of causal certainty the evidence supports (0-4), identify the key biases, confounders, or missing evidence, and restate the claim at an appropriate level of caution.

expected_reasoning_points.point: Is Gene A even the causal gene? LD plus absence of colocalization leaves this open.
Whole-blood eQTL may not be the disease-relevant tissue.
Pathway annotation is descriptive, not causal.
Mouse knockout does not translate directly to humans, and gene knockout is not the same as pharmacological inhibition (direction and degree of effect differ).
Compounding uncertainty: each cross-scale link carries an assumption, so overall confidence is lower than any single link suggests.
A treatment claim requires human direction-of-effect (MR), target-safety/perturbation evidence, and clinical trials.

ideal_cautious_answer: This is a coherent, attractive hypothesis with convergent support, but every cross-scale link carries unproven assumptions, and those uncertainties compound. The evidence supports prioritizing Gene A for further study, not a validated causal mechanism or any claim about treating patients.
common_failure_modes: Accepts the full chain as established
Equates mouse KO with a human drug effect
Fails to note compounding cross-scale uncertainty

resolution.description: Constructed cross-scale vignette; the compounding of link-level uncertainty is the target concept.

notes: Cross-scale items are expected to show the highest overclaiming rates.
